        the computer-readable database, which are being compiled by a group of scientists whose
        names and affiliations appear in the following. In collecting the original data, cooperation
        of researchers in this field was obtained worldwide, including moral support of the
        Committee for International Nuclear Quadrupole Resonance Spectroscopy Symposium.
        The authors extend sincere gratitude to all those people, including graduate students
        and secretaries who contributed to the building ot the database and publishing of the

Preface                             
                                                                       
             The present volume III/31 continues the compilation of nuclear quadrupole resonance
           spectroscopy data of solid substancesw hich started in the year 1988 with volume IIU20. The
           latter covered the literature from the beginning ofnuclear quadrupole resonance spectrosco-
           py in 1951t hrough the end of 1982.I t contained the data published in this period for 8000 sub-
           stances in the solid state, distributed among three subvolumes 111/20a,b , and c.
             Volume III/20 is now supplemented by volume III/31 covering the literature published in
           the period from the end of 1982 through the end of 1989. Included are the data for substances
           studied for the first time, as well as data for substances already present in the previous vol-
           ume III/20 if the data published there could be completed or improved by new studies. In
           total there are 2500 substances in volume 111/31,s howing the scope and significance of
           nuclear quadrupole resonance spectroscopy up to recent times.
             The organization and representation of the data are the same as in the previous volume
           IIIRO. A few, minor modifications are explained in the introduction (chapter 1). Apart from
           these the introduction is a reprint of that in volume 111/20.A lso reprinted from this volume
           are the tables of nuclear quadrupole interaction parameters (chapter 2) which among other
           tables include eigenvalues of the nuclear spin Hamiltonian. Thus volume III/31 can be con-
           sulted without recourse to its predecessor.                 
             For reasons of volume the tables, figures, and indexes are distributed among two subvol-
           umes, a and b. Subvolume 111/3la is presented now while subvolume 111/3lb will follow
           soon.                                                       
             It is a great pleasure for us to thank particularly the authors, who produced camera-ready
           pages of the vast set of data which they have critically selected from among the data in the
           data bank initiated by them. We are strongly indebted to the editorial staff of Landolt-Born-
           stein, especially Dr. W. Finger and Frau D. Rathgeber-Manns, for the great care they showed
           in reviewing and editing of the material submitted. Last but not least our sincere thanks are
           due to Springer-Verlag for the usual high quality of book production.
                                                                       
           Darmstadt, September 1992                     The Editors

                            3.1 Introductory remarks                 
                                                                     
          Numerical data of the nuclear quadrupolc rcsonancc frcqucncics arc prcscntcd in this Chapter. Tables
         arc arranged according to the atomic number of the element, the nuclei of which arc at resonance. With
         the exception of halogens and nitrogen for which there arc so many substances to bc listed, all the data
         for a particular elcmcnt constitute a Table. Data for diffcrcnt nuclear spccics of a given element arc listed
         under the particular substance. Thus, data for 35Cl and 37C1a rc grouped in the same Table. The same
         substance may appear a number of times in diffcrcnt Tables if diffcrcnt elcmcnts in the substance arc
         mcasurcd.                                                   
          The column headings arc almost self-explanatory but for the sake of convcnicncc, they arc listed below.
         Subst. No.                                                  
            The substances arc numbcrcd sequentially within a Table. In the indexes at the end of the present
            volumes, specific substance entries arc dcsignatcd by combination of Table No. and Subst. No.,
            e.g. 3.,.245 to indicate the substance 245 in Table 3. The substancesa rc arranged alphabetically within
            a table.                                                 
         Formula                                                     
            Gross molecular formula is given according to the modified Hill system. The reader is referred to
            a more dctailcd discussion on the molecular formula in Subsection 1.5.1. The water of crystallization
            is separated from the rest of the formula with a dot in bctwecn. Similarly, components of an intermole-
            cular compound arc also scparatcd. Structural formulas for very complex substances will be found
            in Chapter 5 whcrc the structures arc identified by the Table No.-Subst. No. Crystalline modifications
            are identified in accordance to the preference of the author(s) of the original paper; thus “Phase
            I”, “ Form i”, etc. will bc used without attempting to unify the terms.
         Nucl.                                                       
            Nuclear spccics arc given in terms of the elcmcntal symbol and the mass number. If no such data
            is given, it indicates the same spccics as in the prcccding line.
         Mcth.                                                       
            The method of mcasurcmcnt is given using the abbreviations:
            C Continuous wave method                                 
            D Double rcsonancc method                                
            P Pulse method                                           
            M NMR method                                             
            E Other methods                                          
            X Method not dcscribcd in the original paper or not rccordcd in the database at the early stage
              of its building.                                       
         Temp.                                                       
            The tempcraturc at which the mcasurcmcnt was made in the unit of Kelvin. Where this data is
            missing. it indicates the same temperature as in the preceding line. If the temperature is not specified
            in the original document, an interrogation mark, ?, is given.
         Freq.                                                       
            The resonance frequency in the unit of MHz. The frequency values are arranged in decreasing order
            at a given temperature. If thcrc are same numbers in two or more consecutive lines, they indicate
            coincidental multiplets.                                 
         Q.C.C.                                                      
            The quadrupole coupling constant in the unit of MHz. These are the values for the principal z
            axis, (~a~~~)Jh. The sign of Q.C.C. is not given; some of those values may bc negative and when
            it is known to bc negative, a remark is given in the footnote.

       The value of the asymmetry parameter of the quadrupole coupling tensor is given. There are cases
       in which the asymmetry parameter is assumed to be 0 because it could not be determined, as for
       35C1i n a powdered specimen. It is important to note that while the Q.C.C. and the v correspond
       to each other if these values are given in the same line, they do not necessarily correspond to the
       frequency value even if it is in the same line. The reader may use the tables in Sections 2.8 ... 2.10
       to obtain the frequency values that correspond to the Q.C.C. and the q listed.
   Rem.                                                               
       Asterisks are given in this column when there is a remark in the footnote.
   Ref.                                                               
       References to a Table are given at the end of each Table. When there is no entry in this column,
       it indicates the same reference as for the preceding line.     
     A footnote is given for each substance of a page for the substance name, the CAS Registry Number
   (if assigned and known), and remarks. The substance name given here is usually a systematic name with
   exceptions of early entries in which author’s nomenclature was adopted. Even in the latter case,t he systematic
   names are added in the Index of substance names at the end of Subvolume c. The footnote merely serves
   the reader for quick identification of the substance. Please read Section 1.5 about the method for more
   systematic identification of the substances.
